skill.name: developing-genkit-js
skill.description: Develop Genkit applications in Node.js and TypeScript with setup guidance, CLI workflows, examples, and troubleshooting references.
skill.summary: Build and troubleshoot Genkit JS apps with current TypeScript patterns and CLI documentation workflows.
skill.author: genkit-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution and weak-crypto patterns, but review found they are mostly Markdown examples, CLI commands, model names, and setup guidance rather than executable hidden code. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it explicitly instructs agents to install packages, run Genkit and npx commands, use provider APIs, and configure API keys.

medium_findings.title: Agent Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run Genkit, npm, npx, and type-check commands. This is expected for a developer setup skill, but it can modify a user workspace or install packages if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented plainly in the skill and setup workflow. They appear legitimate for Genkit development, but they still cause external command execution and possible dependency installation.

medium_findings.title: Provider API Key Handling Required
medium_findings.description: The documentation tells users to store provider keys in environment variables and reminds them to set a GEMINI_API_KEY. This is normal configuration guidance, but users must avoid exposing secrets in prompts, logs, or generated files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references explicitly mention API keys and environment variables. I found no evidence of secret exfiltration, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Benign Hardcoded URL Examples
low_findings.description: The network findings point to a localhost Developer UI URL and an example.com image URL in sample code. These are documentation examples, not hidden outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URLs are visible in Markdown documentation and are conventional examples. No evidence found that the skill automatically sends data to these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Static Matches Are Model Names
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to match Gemini model names such as gemini-2.5 and gemini-1.5. They are AI model identifiers, not MD5, SHA1, DES, or other cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is clearly used as Genkit model selection guidance. I found no cryptographic implementation or security-sensitive hashing code in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple references include shell commands that an agent may execute during setup, docs lookup, development mode, flow runs, and evaluations. The commands are visible and relevant to the skill purpose.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real because the skill intentionally teaches command execution. The context is developer documentation, so it is not evidence of malicious command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network-Capable AI Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples show provider-backed generation and optional Google Search grounding. These workflows can contact external AI services when the generated application is run.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are transparent and fit Genkit usage. The risk is that users may enable external service calls, not that the skill hides unauthorized network traffic.

content.value_statement: Genkit APIs changed across versions, which can cause setup, type, and runtime errors. This skill gives current Node.js and TypeScript patterns, CLI workflows, and troubleshooting references.
actual_capabilities: Guides Genkit setup for Node.js and TypeScript projects.
Explains current imports, model references, flow definitions, and response access patterns.
Provides CLI commands for docs search, docs reading, dev mode, flow runs, and evaluations.
Shows examples for text generation, structured output, streaming, image generation, and speech generation.
Helps diagnose common Genkit migration, schema, multimodal, and tool registration errors.
limitations: It focuses on Genkit JavaScript and TypeScript, not Python or other SDKs.
It depends on the installed Genkit CLI and current provider documentation.
It does not include full production architecture, deployment, or security hardening guidance.
Some model names and provider features may change after the skill content was published.

use_cases.title: Start a Genkit project
use_cases.description: Set up Genkit files, provider plugins, and a development script in a Node.js or TypeScript app.

use_cases.title: Fix migration errors
use_cases.description: Update older Genkit imports, flow definitions, model references, and response handling to current v1 patterns.

use_cases.title: Prototype AI workflows
use_cases.description: Create examples for generation, structured output, streaming, multimodal output, and provider-backed tools.

prompt_templates.title: Set up Genkit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup in an existing repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Genkit in this Node.js project. Check the existing structure first, ask before installing provider packages, and add only the minimal starter files.

prompt_templates.title: Create a flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Genkit flow for my TypeScript app. Use current Genkit imports, a clear input schema, and a small example I can run from the CLI.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Genkit errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Diagnose this Genkit error. Compare it against the common errors reference, explain the root cause, and make the smallest code change needed.

prompt_templates.title: Design a production workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Genkit design for a production AI workflow. Check flow boundaries, schemas, tool registration, provider configuration, and evaluation strategy.

output_examples.input: Set up Genkit in a new TypeScript service.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the source directory, adds a Genkit initialization module, creates flow and tool folders, adds a dev UI script, and reminds the user to configure provider keys.

output_examples.input: Fix a flow that imports defineFlow from an old package.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that current Genkit defines flows from the Genkit instance, updates imports, and checks response access patterns.
The assistant describes a flow that iterates over stream chunks, awaits the final response, and verifies the command used to run it.

best_practices: Ask before installing packages or running long-lived development commands.
Verify current Genkit behavior with the CLI documentation before changing code.
Keep provider keys in environment variables and avoid writing secrets into source files.
anti_patterns: Using deprecated pre-1.0 Genkit imports or global configuration patterns.
Hardcoding API keys, model credentials, or user secrets in generated examples.
Assuming multimodal, audio, or provider-specific formats without checking current docs.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Genkit applications, flows, tools, and examples in Node.js and TypeScript.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It contains command guidance, but the assistant should ask before installs or disruptive commands.

faq.question: Which providers does it cover?
faq.answer: It defaults to Google AI examples and points users to Genkit docs for other provider plugins.

faq.question: Can it fix older Genkit code?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents common migration issues for imports, flows, models, responses, and streaming.

faq.question: Does it include deployment guidance?
faq.answer: Only limited setup guidance. Production deployment, monitoring, and access control need separate review.

faq.question: Is it safe for marketplace publishing?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning for command execution, provider network access, and API key handling.
